plan_adherence_notes: All 9 plan steps were executed. Step 1: Playwright installed, config created with Chromium-only/webServer/retries — matches plan exactly. Step 2: mock-data.ts and test-fixtures.ts created with typed mock responses and comprehensive route interception. Steps 3-7: All 5 spec files created with the planned test counts (5+5+4+4+3 = 21 tests). Step 8: CI updated with Playwright install and E2E step. Step 9 (run and verify): implementation.json claims verified but tests were only listed, not executed — missing system deps (libnspr4.so) prevented Chromium from launching in both execution and validation environments. One code bug: session-navigation.spec.ts line 6 calls test.use() inside a test body, which is invalid in Playwright (must be at describe level). Minor unrelated diff noise: d3-* deps removed and recharts added in package.json — not part of this task, likely from branch merges.
research_quality_notes: All 8 questions answered with specific file:line evidence. Research correctly identified: Vite proxy config (vite.config.ts:14-22), localStorage session format (session/context.tsx:4-21), API endpoint routing (server.py:4931-5051), WS complexity (3 endpoints with reconnect), CI structure (.github/workflows/ci.yml:1-48). All 7 assumptions verified as CORRECT with appropriate confidence levels. Plan decisions (d1-d7) trace directly back to research answers. Backend port discrepancy (default 3205 vs proxy 8080) correctly flagged as irrelevant with mocking.
question_completeness_notes: 8 questions covered all major architectural decisions: backend approach, data seeding, CI, browsers, session setup, scope per view, WebSocket, file location. Good coverage for the 'happy path first' requirement.
missed_questions: Should E2E tests cover error/failure states (API 500s, network timeouts, malformed responses) or only happy paths? Knowing this upfront would clarify whether the mock fixtures need error response variants.
How should the dev server be handled when port 3000 is already in use? The config uses reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI, but no question was asked about port conflicts during local development.
Should tests assert on specific CSS/visual state (loading spinners, skeleton screens) or only on final rendered content? The app likely has loading states that could cause flaky tests if not handled.
reason: Execution faithfully followed the plan across all 9 steps. Test infrastructure is well-designed: typed mock data importing from actual API types (ensuring type safety), comprehensive route interception covering all /v1/* endpoints plus WebSocket blocking, proper localStorage session injection via addInitScript. 21 tests across 5 spec files use idiomatic Playwright patterns (getByRole, getByText, getByLabel, getByPlaceholder). CI integration added correctly. Two caveats: (1) tests could not be executed in either execution or validation environments due to missing Chromium system dependencies — so actual pass/fail status is unverified, and (2) session-navigation.spec.ts has a test.use() call inside a test body that will fail at runtime. These are fixable issues but not plan-breaking. The overall approach, structure, and code quality are solid.
